questions.questionText: A mechanically ventilated patient in the ICU develops sudden hypotension, elevated peak airway pressures, absent breath sounds on the left, and tracheal deviation to the right. Immediate management?
questions.optionB: Needle decompression of left chest at 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (tension pneumothorax — clinical diagnosis, do NOT wait for CXR)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Tension pneumothorax = clinical diagnosis → immediate needle decompression. Why correct: Classic presentation: hypotension + high airway pressures + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation + distended neck veins = tension pneumothorax. Life-threatening — do NOT wait for imaging. Needle decompression (14G, 2nd ICS MCL) converts tension to simple pneumothorax, then insert chest drain. Why A wrong: NEVER delay treatment for imaging in suspected tension pneumothorax. Why C wrong: Increasing PEEP would worsen pneumothorax. Why D wrong: Fluid bolus won't fix mechanical obstruction of venous return. Clinical relevance: Common in ventilated patients (barotrauma), post-CVC insertion, chest trauma. Exam trap: This is a CLINICAL diagnosis — decompress first, confirm later with CXR. Waiting for CXR = death. Memory aid: 'Tension = Time-critical Tapping required immediately.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Critical Care.

questions.optionB: Suspected infection + acute increase in SOFA score ≥2 points (representing life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sepsis-3 (2016) = infection + organ dysfunction (SOFA ≥2). Why correct: Sepsis-3 moved away from SIRS criteria. Sepsis = suspected/documented infection + acute organ dysfunction (SOFA score ≥2). Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors needed to maintain MAP ≥65 + lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. qSOFA (bedside screening): ≥2 of RR ≥22, altered mental status, SBP ≤100. Why A wrong: SIRS criteria were used in Sepsis-1/2 definitions — too sensitive, not specific (present in many non-infectious conditions). Why C wrong: Blood cultures may be negative in sepsis. Why D wrong: Fever alone is not diagnostic. Clinical relevance: Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures, IV antibiotics, lactate measurement, fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid), vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line). Exam trap: SIRS criteria are STILL important for screening but are no longer the DEFINITION of sepsis. Memory aid: 'Sepsis-3 = SOFA + Suspected infection.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Sepsis.
